title: Curriculum Contrastive Learning for Enzyme Function Prediction
description: We propose CurrEC, a multi-phase curriculum contrastive learning framework that progressively trains enzyme representations from coarse to fine EC classification levels. Unlike single-phase hierarchical approaches (Yim et al., 2024; EnzHier), CurrEC trains sequentially across four phases -- each exclusively at one EC level with checkpoint inheritance -- and uses SupCon-based consistency regularization to preserve coarse-level structure. This produces more structured and discriminative representations than flat or single-phase hierarchical training, with particular improvements for rare enzyme classes.
motivation: Current enzyme function prediction methods either use flat contrastive learning (CLEAN) that ignores the hierarchical structure of EC numbers, or exploit the hierarchy within a single training phase through loss-level weight adjustment (Yim et al., 2024), margin scheduling (EnzHier), multi-level architectures (HIT-EC), or autoregressive prediction (MAPred). None employ multi-phase curriculum learning -- sequential training from easy (coarse EC levels) to hard (fine-grained EC levels) with checkpoint inheritance. This matters because: (1) fine-grained EC classes have extreme class imbalance that coarse-level pre-training mitigates, (2) simultaneously optimizing across ~6000 classes creates a complex loss landscape that sequential refinement smooths, and (3) single-phase methods must balance coarse and fine objectives simultaneously, while curriculum training builds coarse structure first then refines it.
proposed_approach: CurrEC trains a lightweight two-layer MLP projection network on top of frozen ESM-2 (650M) embeddings using a four-phase curriculum. Phase 1 trains with SupCon loss at EC level 1 (7 classes), Phase 2 at level 2 (~70 classes), Phase 3 at level 3 (~300 classes), and Phase 4 at level 4 (~6000 classes). Each phase initializes from the previous checkpoint. The total loss at phase k is: L_k = L_SupCon^(k) + lambda * sum_{l=1}^{k-1} L_SupCon^(l), where the consistency terms are SupCon losses computed with coarser-level labels to prevent catastrophic forgetting. Progressive temperature scheduling (tau_k = tau_base * gamma^(k-1)) adapts discrimination sharpness across phases. At inference, nearest-centroid prediction follows the CLEAN protocol.

related_work.title: Enzyme function prediction using contrastive learning
related_work.authors: Tianhao Yu, Haiyang Cui, Jianan Canal Li, Yunan Luo, Guangde Jiang, Huimin Zhao
related_work.relevance: Introduced supervised contrastive learning for EC prediction (flat SupCon). New-392 F1=0.502, Price-149 F1=0.438. CurrEC extends CLEAN's framework with multi-phase curriculum training.

related_work.title: Hierarchical Contrastive Learning for Enzyme Function Prediction
related_work.authors: Soorin Yim, Doyeong Hwang, Kiyoung Kim, Sehui Han
related_work.venue: ICML 2024 Workshop on ML for Life and Material Science
related_work.relevance: Most closely related: uses hierarchical contrastive loss with gradual weight adjustment for EC hierarchy. However, operates within a single training phase with loss-level weight scheduling. CurrEC differs by using sequential multi-phase training with checkpoint inheritance and explicit SupCon-based consistency regularization.

related_work.title: EnzHier: Accurate Enzyme Function Prediction Through Multi-scale Feature Integration and Hierarchical Contrastive Learning
related_work.authors: Haoyu Duan, Jing Zhu, Qian Liu, Guihua Li
related_work.relevance: Uses U-Net backbone with hierarchical triplet loss and EC-based margin adjustment. Reports 23% higher F1 on CV. Single-phase training with margin adjustment, not curriculum. CurrEC uses SupCon (not triplet) with multi-phase curriculum.

related_work.title: Interpretable Enzyme Function Prediction via Residue-Level Detection
related_work.authors: Zhao Yang, Bing Su, Jiahao Chen, Ji-Rong Wen
related_work.relevance: Detection-based approach with functional queries. New-392 P/R=0.594/0.608, Price-149 R=0.507. Different paradigm (detection vs contrastive), does not use curriculum.

related_work.title: Autoregressive enzyme function prediction with multi-scale multi-modality fusion
related_work.authors: Dingyi Rong, Bozitao Zhong, Wenzhuo Zheng, Liang Hong, Ning Liu
related_work.relevance: Current SOTA on benchmarks: New-392 F1=0.610, Price-149 F1=0.493. Uses 3D structure and autoregressive prediction. Different paradigm from contrastive learning; CurrEC is sequence-only.

related_work.title: Trustworthy prediction of enzyme commission numbers using a hierarchical interpretable transformer
related_work.authors: Louis Dumontet, So-Ra Han, Tae-Jin Oh, Mingon Kang
related_work.relevance: Four-level hierarchical transformer. Trains all levels jointly. CurrEC uses simpler architecture with curriculum training.

related_work.title: Improved enzyme functional annotation prediction using contrastive learning with structural inference
related_work.authors: Yuxin Yang, Abby Jerger, Song Feng, Zixu Wang, Christina Brasfield, Margaret S. Cheung, Jeremy Zucker, Qiang Guan
related_work.relevance: Extends CLEAN with structural contact maps via GNN. Flat contrastive training without curriculum.

related_work.title: Supervised Contrastive Learning
related_work.authors: Prannay Khosla, Piotr Teterwak, Chen Wang, Aaron Sarna, Yonglong Tian, Phillip Isola, Aaron Maschinot, Ce Liu, Dilip Krishnan
related_work.relevance: Foundation for the SupCon loss used in CurrEC at each curriculum phase and in consistency regularization terms.

related_work.title: Curriculum Learning
related_work.authors: Yoshua Bengio, Jerome Louradour, Ronan Collobert, Jason Weston
related_work.relevance: Foundational work on easy-to-hard training ordering. Directly inspires CurrEC's coarse-to-fine curriculum strategy.

related_work.title: Evolutionary-scale prediction of atomic-level protein structure with a language model
related_work.authors: Zeming Lin, Halil Akin, Roshan Rao, Brian Hie, Zhongkai Zhu, Wenting Lu, et al.
related_work.relevance: Provides ESM-2, the protein language model backbone used for embedding extraction in CurrEC.

hypothesis: Progressive coarse-to-fine contrastive training across EC hierarchy levels, with checkpoint inheritance and SupCon-based consistency regularization, produces more structured and discriminative enzyme representations than both flat training and single-phase hierarchical training (Yim et al., 2024; EnzHier), leading to improved prediction accuracy especially for rare enzyme classes with fewer than 10 training examples.
success_criteria: CurrEC achieves higher macro-F1 than flat SupCon baseline (same architecture, no curriculum) on New-392 and Price-149 benchmarks
CurrEC outperforms joint hierarchical SupCon (single-phase, all levels simultaneously with weighted losses) to isolate the curriculum effect from hierarchy awareness
CurrEC improves F1 on rare EC classes (n < 10 training examples) by at least 5% relative to flat training
Coarse-to-fine curriculum order outperforms reverse (fine-to-coarse) and random curriculum orders
Consistency regularization (lambda > 0) demonstrably prevents forgetting of coarse-level structure versus lambda = 0
CurrEC matches or exceeds CLEAN performance on standard benchmarks
